cards_stock.narrative: The credit card market hit an all-time high of 119.4M cards in Apr 2026, growing 8.1% YoY — the fastest in 4 months — while the debit card market underwent a massive 21.4M card purge, the largest in this dataset, driving YoY growth down to 2.8%, the slowest in 16 months. The credit oligopoly is loosening as mid-tier banks captured 83% of new issuance (664K of 802K cards), while the debit oligopoly tightened sharply as PSBs and privates gained 1.3pp and 0.7pp share respectively by shedding fewer inactive cards than payment banks (which lost 2.3pp). Private banks now hold 71.0% of credit cards and 25.8% of debit cards — the latter a record high — confirming they are capturing share in both markets despite the purge. The Apr 2026 debit purge is a one-time cleanup event, not a structural contraction — expect the market to stabilise at 1.02B–1.03B cards while credit continues its 8%+ growth trajectory through Q2 2026.

chain: The top three banks hold 67,880,987 cards out of 119,436,129 total — a 56.8% share, unchanged from prior period.
HDFC added 138,304 cards while the market added 802,452 total — the top three captured 17% of new issuance, down from 35% prior period.
The mid-tier added 664,148 cards (83% of growth) vs 329,000 cards prior period (35% of growth) — the shift to mid-tier issuance has accelerated sharply.
AU Small Finance Bank shifted from weakening to strengthening, joining 27 other banks in expansion mode — breadth is widening as the oligopoly's grip loosens.
cc-bank-scan.inference: The oligopoly is losing control of new issuance — the top three captured only 17% of the 802,452 new cards in Apr 2026, down from 35% in Mar 2026, confirming mid-tier PSBs and smaller privates are now driving 83% of market growth. The 56.8% concentration is frozen, but the velocity has shifted: mid-tier banks added 664,148 cards vs 329,000 prior period, a 2× acceleration. AU Small Finance Bank's status flip to strengthening signals the mid-tier is entering a sustained expansion phase. The top three's share will crack if this issuance pattern holds for two more periods.

infrastructure.narrative: India's digital acceptance infrastructure has bifurcated decisively: UPI QR codes hit a record 770.8M in Apr 2026, adding 9.6M codes and growing 16.4% YoY, while POS terminals contracted to 11.45M, losing 322K units and growing just 1.5% YoY — the weakest rate on record. The UPI QR-to-POS ratio surged to 67.3×, a new high, as POS deployment stalled and UPI QR scaled. The POS network has now failed to sustain expansion beyond four months twice in six periods, confirming it is a legacy channel losing ground to QR-based payments. Lenders must recalibrate merchant credit strategies for a UPI-first market — POS-dependent acquiring models and MDR-linked economics are structurally challenged, while UPI-enabled merchant segments represent the growth opportunity.

credit_card_txn.narrative: Credit card transaction flows show a two-speed market: the 'other' category (tap-to-pay, transit, merchant-initiated) is still scaling explosively — ₹92,986 Cr value and 3.42M volume in Apr 2026, both new records, with YoY growth at 492.8% and 299.8% respectively despite four months of deceleration. Meanwhile, POS channels are volatile: Apr value fell ₹4,622 Cr to ₹73,849 Cr and volume dropped 845K to 27.6 Cr, erasing much of Mar's surge and confirming the prior month was a spike, not a new baseline. POS share rose marginally to 37.4% (value) and 49.2% (volume), but remains structurally below the Nov 2025 peaks — ecommerce retains majority share in transaction count for the fourth straight month. The strategic implication: offline retail is growing steadily (8.8% value YoY, 20.8% volume YoY) but losing ground to faster-growing online channels, while the 'other' category is embedding as a permanent fixture despite its sub-1% share. Lenders should plan for a gradual shift toward online-dominant channel mix and monitor whether 'other' category growth sustains above 400% YoY as the base matures.

chain: Other transaction volume reached 3.42M in Apr 2026, up 72K from Mar 2026's 3.34M.
The category has grown every month since Nov 2024, compounding from 145K to 3.42M — a 24× surge in 18 months.
This sustained trajectory confirms the new transaction type is embedding structurally in cardholder behaviour, not plateauing.
cc-other-vol-abs.inference: The Mar recovery was not a one-off — Apr's 72K gain extends the growth streak to three months and sets a new record. This category is still scaling rapidly despite the maturing base. Lenders should monitor whether monthly gains hold above 50K as the category approaches 4M transactions.

debit_card_txn.narrative: Debit card cash withdrawals at POS terminals are in terminal collapse. Apr 2026 value of ₹15.1M and volume of 8,094 transactions are both all-time lows, down 75% and 87% respectively from Jan 2024 peaks. The positive 6.0% YoY value growth is purely a base effect — comparing two structurally weak periods — not a recovery. The channel has never registered a measurable share of debit card economics (0.00% of value and volume across 28 months) and is now operationally negligible. Issuers should complete sunset immediately: maintaining infrastructure for 8,000 monthly transactions is economically indefensible and this feature has no strategic future in the debit card mix.
